original_hypothesis.title: closeMarket Loop Repeatedly Processes Same Unpaid Batch Without Progress
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket(), the loop `for (uint256 i; i < numBatches; i++)` processes unpaid batches by calling _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch, which always reads `first()` from the unpaidBatches queue. If the first batch cannot be fully paid (due to insufficient liquidity after rounding), it remains at the front of the queue. The loop continues iterating numBatches times, re-processing the same batch with progressively less (or zero) liquidity. This wastes gas proportional to numBatches without making forward progress.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Compare with repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches which has `while (i++ < numBatches && availableLiquidity > 0)` - the extra `availableLiquidity > 0` guard prevents useless iterations. closeMarket lacks this optimization.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop with numBatches iterations always calling first() without checking if progress is made; compare with WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol:184 which checks availableLiquidity > 0

preconditions: First batch in queue cannot be fully paid due to liquidity constraints or rounding

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded loop processing same element without progress check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always reads first() - if batch not fully paid, it stays at front, loop processes it again with zero additional payment
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `availableLiquidity > 0` check in loop condition, no break on zero-progress iteration

analysis_questions: What is the gas cost per iteration when processing a batch with zero available liquidity?
Can a malicious lender create enough unpaid batches to make closeMarket exceed block gas limit?
Does the loop always terminate or can it cause OOG?
validation_requirements: Create market with many unpaid batches and attempt closeMarket
Measure gas usage per loop iteration

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A malicious lender queues many small withdrawals at intervals matching the withdrawal batch duration, creating numerous unpaid batches (especially if withdrawal batch duration is very small). When the borrower attempts to close the market, the loop iterates over all batches but gets stuck processing the first one repeatedly, potentially exceeding block gas limit.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Borrower unable to close market due to gas exhaustion; continues paying interest
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Borrower could use repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches to clear batches first

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: closeMarket with large numbers of unpaid batches

validation_result.mechanism: missing loop guard causes wasted iterations
validation_result.impact_summary: closeMarket loop iterates numBatches times without checking if availableLiquidity > 0, causing the same first unpaid batch to be re-processed with zero liquidity for all remaining iterations, wasting gas proportional to numBatches.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The closeMarket function at WildcatMarket.sol:223-230 contains a for loop that iterates numBatches times, calling _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch on each iteration. Unlike repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches (WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol:184) which uses `while (i++ < numBatches && availableLiquidity > 0)`, closeMarket lacks the `availableLiquidity > 0` guard.

_processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always reads first() from the unpaidBatches queue. If a batch is not fully paid (scaledAmountBurned < scaledTotalAmount), it remains at the front of the queue. When availableLiquidity reaches 0, subsequent iterations call _applyWithdrawalBatchPayment with 0 liquidity, resulting in scaledAmountBurned=0 and normalizedAmountPaid=0. The batch stays at front, and the loop continues doing storage reads/writes and emitting events with zero values for all remaining iterations.

Additionally, closeMarket uses unchecked subtraction `availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid` instead of satSub. If rounding causes normalizedAmountPaid > availableLiquidity, this would revert with arithmetic underflow, making closeMarket fail entirely.
